message:
Add empty path_entries early return in compaction and re-serialize beads

- compaction.rs: Add early return None when path_entries is empty,
  complementing the existing no-op guard for empty summarization inputs.
  Prevents potential index-out-of-bounds on completely fresh sessions.
- .beads/issues.jsonl: Re-serialization from concurrent br sync activity.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.2","title":"Hostcall ABI: Wire ExtensionMessage host_call -> host_result (Protocol Adapter)","description":"# Goal
Make the protocol types in `src/extensions.rs` real at runtime by implementing an adapter that:

- accepts `ExtensionMessage(type=host_call)`
- dispatches it via the shared hostcall dispatcher
- emits `ExtensionMessage(type=host_result)`

# Background
- `ExtensionMessage::parse_and_validate()` exists but is not used by any runtime component.
- Having protocol message wiring is useful for:
  - harness/testing (treat hostcall ABI as an artifact)
  - future external runtimes (Tier C process IPC) without re-implementing dispatch logic

# Scope
- Implement a small "protocol adapter" surface (library API), not necessarily a user-facing CLI:
  - `fn handle_extension_message(msg: ExtensionMessage, ctx: ...) -> Vec<ExtensionMessage>`
  - For now, only `host_call` is required; other message types can be `invalid_request`.
- Ensure **streaming-ready** behavior:
  - Return value is a `Vec<ExtensionMessage>` to allow multiple `host_result` chunks later.
  - Initial implementation can emit exactly one `host_result` with `chunk=None`.

# Required Semantics
- The adapter must:
  - preserve `call_id`
  - enforce capability mismatch -> `invalid_request`
  - enforce taxonomy codes
  - never emit raw params in logs

# Tests
- Unit tests:
  - parse+validate `host_call` JSON -> dispatch -> `host_result` JSON validates
  - invalid JSON / invalid version / unknown method -> `error` or `host_result` invalid_request
  - denied-by-policy -> `host_result` denied

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ExtensionMessage(type=host_call)` can be round-tripped through adapter to `host_result`.
- [ ] All error branches produce valid `HostResultPayload` with taxonomy codes.
- [ ] Adapter is decoupled enough to be used by future process-based extension runners.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","protocol"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:09.534702397Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.3","title":"Hostcall ABI: JS Runtime Uses Shared Dispatcher + Taxonomy","description":"# Goal
Refactor JS-tier hostcalls (`pi.*` -> `HostcallRequest`) so they execute through the **shared HostCallPayload -> HostResultPayload dispatcher**.

# Background
- Today JS-tier hostcalls flow through `HostcallRequest`/`HostcallOutcome` and return ad-hoc codes like `tool_error`/`SHUTDOWN`.
- The hostcall ABI spec requires **fixed error taxonomy** and shared semantics across JS and WASM.

# Scope
- Create a lossless conversion:
  - `HostcallRequest` -> `extensions::HostCallPayload`:
    - `method` from `request.method()`
    - `params` from `request.params_for_hash()` (or equivalent canonical params shape)
    - `capability` derived (NOT caller-controlled)
    - `timeout_ms` derived from request options where applicable
  - dispatcher output `HostResultPayload` -> JS completion `HostcallOutcome`:
    - success => `HostcallOutcome::Success(output)`
    - error => `HostcallOutcome::Error { code, message }` where `code` is **one of** taxonomy codes only
- Preserve existing deterministic scheduling behavior:
  - completions must still enqueue as macrotasks; no synchronous re-entrancy.

# Required Semantics
- JS hostcall logs must match WASM logs for equivalent calls:
  - `host_call.start/end`, `policy.decision`, `params_hash`, `duration_ms`.
- Remove/translate non-taxonomy codes:
  - `tool_error` -> `io` (or `internal` when clearly a bug)
  - `SHUTDOWN` -> `internal` (or `invalid_request`) but never a new code

# Tests
- Update/add unit tests covering:
  - tool failure maps to taxonomy
  - manager shutdown path maps cleanly (no `SHUTDOWN`)
  - unknown tool => `invalid_request`
  - denied-by-policy => `denied`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] JS tier only emits error codes in `timeout|denied|io|invalid_request|internal`.
- [ ] Existing ext_conformance + event_loop determinism tests still pass.
- [ ] Logs remain deterministic and do not include raw params.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","js"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:56:34.065311493Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.4","title":"Hostcall ABI: Cross-Runtime Parity Tests (JS/WASM/Protocol)","description":"# Goal
Add tests that prove the hostcall ABI is stable and identical across:
- JS runtime hostcalls
- WASM hostcalls
- protocol adapter (`ExtensionMessage host_call -> host_result`)

# Why
Without parity tests, JS/WASM divergence will regress silently, undermining:
- security claims (policy enforcement)
- compatibility claims (extensions "just work")
- conformance harness reliability

# Scope
Create a small canonical suite of host_call examples (method+params) and assert:
1) All three paths return a `host_result` with:
   - the same `is_error` value
   - the same taxonomy error code (if error)
   - output object shape consistent with the method
2) Logs:
   - `host_call.start` and `host_call.end` exist
   - include `params_hash`
   - never include raw params

# Canonical Cases (Minimum)
- `tool`:
  - read-like (`grep`) success
  - unknown tool invalid_request
- `http`:
  - denied host -> denied
  - timeout -> timeout (use deterministic clock/harness)
- `exec`:
  - denied by policy -> denied
- `session`:
  - when session handle not configured -> denied or internal (taxonomy, deterministic)
- `ui`:
  - when UI handler not configured -> denied or timeout (taxonomy, deterministic)

# Implementation Notes
- Prefer table-driven tests.
- Avoid network flakiness: use local TCP listener like existing wasm-host tests.
- Ensure wasm-host tests run under `--features wasm-host`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tests fail if any runtime emits a non-taxonomy code.
- [ ] Tests fail if JS/WASM diverge for the same host_call.
- [ ] CI path includes both default and `wasm-host` feature test runs.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","hostcall","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-nom.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:59.527965561Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-1uy.1.5","title":"Hostcall ABI: Consolidate Duplicate HostCallPayload/HostResultPayload Types","description":"# Goal
Remove (or strictly contain) the duplicated hostcall protocol type definitions:
- `crate::extensions::{HostCallPayload, HostResultPayload, ...}`
- `crate::connectors::{HostCallPayload, HostResultPayload, ...}`

# Background
We currently maintain two parallel type graphs for the same conceptual ABI. This is a long-term drift hazard:
- fields can diverge (e.g., streaming/backpressure)
- error taxonomy mapping can diverge
- serialization/validation rules can diverge

# Scope
Choose one canonical home for the hostcall ABI types and make the other:
- a re-export, OR
- a thin newtype wrapper, OR
- a conversion layer with exhaustive tests

Constraints:
- Keep public API stability internal to the crate (no backwards-compat shims required).
- Ensure schema validation and unit tests remain intact.

# Tests
- Compile-time: no duplicate definitions used across modules.
- Runtime: round-trip serialize/deserialize across any boundary still works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] There is exactly one source-of-truth for hostcall payload/result structs.
- [ ] All call sites compile and tests pass.
- [ ] Any conversion layer has unit tests that would catch field drift.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:23.972343512Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:23.972343512Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cleanup","extensions","hostcall"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:23.972343512Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:23.972343512Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1v10","title":"Phase 0: Environment and Runtime Setup","description":"# Phase 0: Environment and Runtime Setup

## Purpose
Before we can do ANY differential testing, we need BOTH runtimes working and verified independently. This phase ensures:
1. The pi-mono TypeScript runtime can load and run extensions via Bun
2. The Rust QuickJS runtime can load and run extensions
3. A deterministic test environment exists (no randomness, fixed timestamps)

## Why This Is Phase 0
Everything else depends on this. If either runtime cannot load extensions, differential testing is impossible. We discovered Bun 1.3.8 is available at /home/ubuntu/.bun/bin/bun. The pi-mono source lives at legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/. We need to verify it builds and its extension loader works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- pi-mono extension loader.ts can load the hello.ts extension and produce output
- Rust QuickJS runtime can load the hello.ts extension and produce output
- Both outputs can be captured as structured JSON
- Deterministic mode eliminates timestamps, random values, cwd-dependent paths","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:14:07.671337047Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T17:36:15.918993495Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T17:36:15.918905561Z","close_reason":"Phase 0 complete: pi-mono loads extensions via Bun/jiti (tests/ext_conformance/ts_oracle/load_extension.ts), Rust QuickJS runtime loads extensions (tests/ext_conformance_diff.rs), both produce structured JSON, all 60 official extensions validated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1v10","depends_on_id":"bd-3odv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:14:07.724213001Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":309,"issue_id":"bd-1v10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"KEY DISCOVERY: Bun 1.3.8 is available at /home/ubuntu/.bun/bin/bun, which also provides a node shim. pi-mono uses jiti for extension loading (not native Bun module resolution). The virtualModules mechanism in loader.ts maps package names to bundled versions (@sinclair/typebox, @mariozechner/pi-agent-core, etc.). In development mode it uses aliases instead. For our harness, we need to run in development mode (not Bun binary mode) so that jiti resolves imports via filesystem.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:27:14Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1v8m","title":"Security suite: wasm import gating (PiWasm)","description":"# Goal
Ensure wasm executed via PiWasm cannot access OS without explicit connector imports.

# Scope
- Deny side-effectful imports without capability.
- Ensure traps/errors are deterministic and logged.

# Dependencies
- Blocked by PiWasm implementation under bd-1ry.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:15:12.498889128Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T03:15:12.505632917Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1v8m","depends_on_id":"bd-193","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:15:12.505607820Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1ve4","title":"CI: add cross-platform build/test matrix (linux/macOS/windows)","description":"# Goal
Increase release confidence by running CI on all target platforms (at minimum build + unit tests), and document any platform-specific exclusions.

# Background
The current CI workflow only runs on `ubuntu-latest`. That’s good for fast iteration but insufficient for shipping binaries.

# Scope
- Add a GitHub Actions matrix over:
  - `ubuntu-latest`
  - `macos-latest`
  - `windows-latest`
- For each OS:
  - `cargo fmt --check`
  - `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
  - `cargo test --all-targets`

# Platform Considerations
- System deps:
  - `fd` and `rg` are required/optional for tool tests; install equivalents per OS.
- Path dependencies:
  - CI currently clones sibling repos (asupersync/rich_rust/charmed_rust/sqlmodel_rust). Ensure the same approach works on macOS/Windows.
- VCR playback mode:
  - Keep `VCR_MODE=playback` for determinism.

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ci.yml` runs on Linux/macOS/Windows.
- [ ] Any OS-specific skips are explicit and justified (no silent test omissions).
- [ ] Failures include enough logs to debug platform-specific issues.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss
[ ] Unit tests cover core success/failure + edge cases for this bead (add regression coverage if applicable)
[ ] Integration/E2E script exercises the end-to-end path (or explicitly marks N/A with rationale in notes) and emits detailed JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9
[ ] Logs include inputs, outputs, timing, and redaction summary; artifacts list is deterministic
[ ] Any new fixtures/golden outputs are deterministic and documented
[ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test
[ ] Docs/fixtures updated if behavior or UX changes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T21:21:48.739486913Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T19:27:15.998454113Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T00:29:34.304318188Z","close_reason":"Cross-platform CI matrix in .github/workflows/ci.yml (commit 292102c)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ve4","depends_on_id":"bd-gqtd","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T00:00:00Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2uh1.1","title":"Extension registry protocol spec and local index schema","description":"# Goal
Design the extension registry protocol — how extension metadata is fetched from remote sources (GitHub, npm), stored locally, and queried for search/browse operations.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already knows how to install from npm and GitHub. But there is no structured index of AVAILABLE extensions. Users must know exact package names. This task designs the metadata layer that enables discovery.

# Deliverables

1. Remote data sources:
   - GitHub: Search GitHub repos with topic "pi-extension" via GitHub API
   - npm: Search npm registry for packages with "pi-extension" keyword
   - Curated list: static JSON manifest in a Pi-maintained repo (for quality-vetted extensions)

2. Local index schema (SQLite or JSON):
   {
     "version": 1,
     "last_refreshed": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z",
     "extensions": [
       {
         "id": "auto-commit-on-exit",
         "name": "Auto Commit on Exit",
         "description": "Automatically commits changes when Pi session ends",
         "version": "1.2.0",
         "source": { "type": "npm", "package": "@user/pi-auto-commit" },
         "author": "username",
         "license": "MIT",
         "tags": ["git", "automation", "lifecycle"],
         "conformance_tier": 1,
         "pi_version_range": ">=0.5.0",
         "downloads_30d": 1500,
         "last_updated": "2026-01-10T00:00:00Z",
         "readme_url": "https://...",
         "repository_url": "https://..."
       }
     ]
   }

3. Refresh strategy:
   - On first "pi search": fetch and cache index
   - Auto-refresh if cache older than 24 hours
   - Manual refresh: "pi update-index"
   - Incremental: only fetch new/updated since last refresh

4. Search algorithm:
   - Fuzzy text match on name + description + tags
   - Weighted scoring: name match (3x) > tag match (2x) > description match (1x)
   - Sort by relevance * quality_signal (conformance tier, downloads)

# Files to Create/Modify
- New: src/extension_index.rs (index management, search, refresh)
- src/package_manager.rs: Wire index into install flow
- src/config.rs: Add index cache path

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Schema documented in docs/extension-registry.md
- [ ] Index refresh from GitHub + npm sources works
- [ ] Fuzzy search returns relevant results
- [ ] Cache invalidation after 24h
- [ ] Index stored in ~/.pi/agent/extension-index.json","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discovery","extensions","protocol"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2uh1.2","title":"pi search CLI command — search available extensions by keyword","description":"# Goal
Implement the "pi search <query>" CLI command that searches the extension index and displays results in a rich terminal table.

# Background
This is the primary user-facing discovery command. It queries the local extension index (built by the registry protocol spec task) and presents results in a scannable format.

# Usage
  pi search git          # Search for git-related extensions
  pi search "auto commit" # Phrase search
  pi search --tag provider # Filter by tag
  pi search --sort downloads # Sort by popularity
  pi search --limit 20     # Limit results

# Output Format (rich_rust table)
  Name              Version  Description                        Downloads  Tier
  auto-commit       1.2.0    Auto commit on session exit         1,500     T1
  git-branch-hook   0.5.0    Switch branches via /git command      890     T2
  git-stats         2.0.1    Show git statistics in session        650     T1

  3 extensions found. Install with: pi install <name>

# Implementation Plan
1. Add "search" subcommand to clap CLI (src/cli.rs)
2. In main.rs, route to search handler
3. Search handler:
   a. Load or refresh extension index
   b. Apply fuzzy search with query
   c. Apply filters (--tag, --tier, --source)
   d. Sort results (--sort: relevance (default), downloads, name, updated)
   e. Render rich_rust table with columns: Name, Version, Description, Downloads, Tier
   f. Print install hint at bottom

4. Auto-refresh:
   - If index doesnt exist or is >24h old, refresh before searching
   - Show "Refreshing extension index..." message during refresh
   - Cache refreshed index for subsequent searches

# Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs: Add search subcommand with args
- src/main.rs: Route search subcommand
- New: src/extension_search.rs or add to extension_index.rs

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "pi search git" returns relevant results
- [ ] Results sorted by relevance by default
- [ ] --tag filter works
- [ ] --sort flag works (relevance, downloads, name, updated)
- [ ] Rich table output with colors
- [ ] Install hint shown at bottom
- [ ] Auto-refresh of stale index
- [ ] Graceful error on network failure (show cached results if available)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:49.290045657Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:49.290045657Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","discovery","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:49.290045657Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2uh1.3","title":"pi info CLI command — rich extension detail view with README rendering","description":"# Goal
Implement "pi info <extension>" CLI command that shows detailed information about an extension including rendered README, capabilities, compatibility status, and install instructions.

# Usage
  pi info auto-commit-on-exit

# Output Format
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ auto-commit-on-exit v1.2.0               │
  │ by username • MIT License                │
  │ Conformance: Tier 1 (fully tested)       │
  │ Pi compatibility: >=0.5.0                │
  │ Downloads (30d): 1,500                   │
  │ Last updated: 2026-01-10                 │
  │ Tags: git, automation, lifecycle         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ README                                    │
  │                                           │
  │ Automatically commits your changes when   │
  │ a Pi session ends. Configurable via...    │
  │                                           │
  │ ## Configuration                          │
  │ ...                                       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Capabilities Required                     │
  │ • exec: git commands                     │
  │ • tool/read: .git/config                 │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Install: pi install npm:@user/pi-auto... │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Implementation Plan
1. Add "info" subcommand to clap CLI
2. Info handler:
   a. Look up extension in index by name/id
   b. Fetch README from source (GitHub API or npm registry)
   c. Parse extension manifest for capabilities
   d. Render rich_rust panel with sections

3. README rendering:
   - Fetch raw markdown from GitHub (raw.githubusercontent.com) or npm readme field
   - Render using glamour (terminal markdown renderer)
   - Truncate to terminal height with "Press q to exit, scroll with arrows" if long
   - Cache fetched READMEs locally (1 hour TTL)

4. Capability extraction:
   - Parse extension source or manifest for hostcall usage
   - Display human-readable capability list

# Files to Modify
- src/cli.rs: Add info subcommand
- src/main.rs: Route info subcommand
- Extension index module: Add detail fetch methods

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] "pi info <name>" shows formatted extension details
- [ ] README rendered as terminal markdown
- [ ] Capabilities listed if available
- [ ] Install command shown at bottom
- [ ] Graceful handling of missing README or unavailable extension
- [ ] Cached README to avoid repeated fetches","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:02.776254423Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:02.776254423Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","discovery","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:02.776254423Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-2uh1.4","title":"Curated extension collections and category taxonomy","description":"# Goal
Define the category taxonomy for extensions and create curated collections (featured, popular by category, starter packs) that help users discover relevant extensions.

# Background
Raw search only works when users know what to look for. Categories and collections enable browsing — "show me all provider extensions" or "what are the most popular automation extensions?" This is how every extension marketplace (VSCode, Chrome Web Store, Obsidian) drives discovery beyond search.

# Deliverables

1. Category taxonomy:
   - providers: Custom LLM providers (OpenAI, local models, etc.)
   - tools: Additional tools beyond the built-in 7
   - automation: Lifecycle hooks, auto-commit, scheduled tasks
   - git: Git-related extensions (branch management, commit helpers)
   - formatting: Output formatting, rendering, themes
   - data: Data processing, analysis, transformation
   - testing: Test runners, coverage reporters
   - security: Audit, scanning, policy enforcement

2. Curated collections:
   - "Getting Started": 5 essential extensions for new users
   - "Power User": 10 extensions for advanced workflows
   - "Provider Pack": All custom provider extensions
   - "DevOps Toolkit": CI/CD, deployment, monitoring extensions

3. Manifest format for collections:
   {
     "id": "getting-started",
     "name": "Getting Started",
     "description": "Essential extensions for new Pi users",
     "extensions": ["auto-commit-on-exit", "git-branch-hook", ...]
   }

4. CLI commands:
   - pi search --category providers   # Browse by category
   - pi collections                    # List available collections
   - pi collections getting-started   # Show collection details

# Files to Modify
- Extension index module: Add category and collection support
- src/cli.rs: Add collections subcommand
- New: data/collections.json (curated collection definitions)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Category taxonomy covers all known extension types in corpus
- [ ] At least 3 curated collections defined
- [ ] pi search --category works
- [ ] pi collections command shows available collections

+{"id":"bd-2uh1.5","title":"Quality signals computation and display for extension discovery","description":"# Goal
Compute and display quality signals for extensions in search results and info views, helping users assess extension reliability before installing.

# Background
Not all extensions are equal. Some are well-tested (conformance tier 1), actively maintained, and widely used. Others are experimental, abandoned, or incompatible. Quality signals help users make informed decisions — like star ratings on app stores or crate download counts on crates.io.

# Quality Signals

1. Conformance Tier (from Pi conformance test suite):
   - T1: Fully tested, all assertions pass
   - T2: Mostly tested, minor issues
   - T3: Partially tested, some features fail
   - Untested: No conformance data

2. Maintenance Status:
   - Active: Updated within 90 days
   - Maintained: Updated within 1 year
   - Stale: Not updated in 1+ year
   - Abandoned: Not updated in 2+ years, no response to issues

3. Compatibility Score (from compat scanner):
   - Green: 100% compatible, no Node.js dependencies
   - Yellow: 90%+ compatible, minor shim gaps
   - Red: <90% compatible, significant issues

4. Popularity Metrics:
   - npm downloads (30d) if from npm
   - GitHub stars if from GitHub
   - Install count from Pi telemetry (future)

5. Composite Quality Score (0-100):
   - 40% conformance tier
   - 25% maintenance status
   - 20% compatibility score
   - 15% popularity metrics

# Display Format
In search results table: colored badge (green/yellow/red) + numeric score
In info view: detailed breakdown of each signal

# Implementation Plan
1. Compute signals at index refresh time
2. Store in extension index entry
3. Sort by composite score in search results (default)
4. Display badge in search table column
5. Detailed breakdown in pi info output

# Files to Modify
- Extension index module: Add quality computation
- Search/info display code: Add quality columns/sections

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 signal types computed for indexed extensions
- [ ] Composite score calculated with documented weights
- [ ] Quality badge visible in search results
- [ ] Detailed breakdown in pi info
- [ ] Score updates on index refresh","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:28.098522967Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:28.098522967Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discovery","extensions","quality"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:28.098522967Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2uhs","title":"Task: Implement Exec Hostcall Handler (pi.exec)","description":"# Task: Implement Exec Hostcall Handler

## Objective

Implement the handler for HostcallKind::Exec that routes pi.exec() calls to shell command execution.

## Background

When an extension calls pi.exec("git", ["status"], {cwd: "/path"}), it should execute the command and return stdout/stderr/exitCode. This is similar to the bash tool but with more granular control.

## TypeScript Reference (loader.ts:215-216)

```typescript
exec(command: string, args: string[], options?: ExecOptions) {
    return execCommand(command, args, options?.cwd ?? cwd, options);
}
```

## Implementation

```rust
impl ExtensionDispatcher {
    /// Handle pi.exec(command, args, options) hostcalls.
    /// 
    /// Executes a shell command with optional working directory and timeout.
    /// Unlike the bash tool, this provides direct command execution without
    /// shell interpretation (no pipes, redirects, etc. unless explicitly using sh -c).
    async fn dispatch_exec(
        &self,
        cmd: &str,
        args: &[String],
        opts: ExecOptions,
    ) -> HostcallOutcome {
        use std::process::Stdio;
        use asupersync::Command;
        
        let cwd = opts.cwd
            .map(PathBuf::from)
            .unwrap_or_else(|| self.cwd.clone());
        
        let timeout = opts.timeout_ms.unwrap_or(120_000); // Default 2 min
        
        // Build command
        let mut command = Command::new(cmd);
        command
            .args(args)
            .current_dir(&cwd)
            .stdin(Stdio::null())
            .stdout(Stdio::piped())
            .stderr(Stdio::piped());
        
        // Set environment if provided
        if let Some(env) = opts.env {
            for (k, v) in env {
                command.env(k, v);
            }
        }
        
        // Execute with timeout
        let result = match tokio::time::timeout(
            Duration::from_millis(timeout),
            command.output(),
        ).await {
            Ok(Ok(output)) => {
                let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).to_string();
                let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).to_string();
                let exit_code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
                
                HostcallOutcome::Success(serde_json::json!({
                    "stdout": stdout,
                    "stderr": stderr,
                    "exitCode": exit_code,
                    "signal": output.status.signal(),
                }))
            }
            Ok(Err(e)) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "EXEC_ERROR".to_string(),
                message: e.to_string(),
            },
            Err(_) => HostcallOutcome::Error {
                code: "TIMEOUT".to_string(),
                message: format!("Command timed out after {}ms", timeout),
            },
        };
        
        result
    }
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]
pub struct ExecOptions {
    pub cwd: Option<String>,
    pub timeout_ms: Option<u64>,
    pub env: Option<HashMap<String, String>>,
}
```

## Security Considerations

1. **Path Traversal**: Validate cwd is within allowed directories
2. **Command Injection**: Args are passed directly, not through shell
3. **Resource Limits**: Enforce timeout, consider memory limits
4. **Capability Check**: Requires "exec" capability in extension manifest

## Differences from Bash Tool

| Aspect | pi.exec() | bash tool |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Shell interpretation | None (direct exec) | Full shell |
| Arguments | Array of args | Single command string |
| Pipes/redirects | Not supported | Supported |
| Use case | Precise commands | Complex scripts |

## Testing

1. Unit test: Simple command (echo)
2. Unit test: Command with args
3. Unit test: Custom cwd
4. Unit test: Timeout handling
5. Unit test: Non-zero exit code
6. Integration test: Real command execution

## Dependencies

- Depends on: bd-389q (ExtensionDispatcher struct)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] dispatch_exec() method implemented
- [ ] Command execution with args works
- [ ] Custom cwd respected
- [ ] Timeout handling works
- [ ] stdout/stderr/exitCode returned
- [ ] Unit tests pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:53:09.935871275Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T21:06:49.420847034Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T21:06:49.420776342Z","close_reason":"Implemented HostcallKind::Exec dispatch in ExtensionDispatcher (Command spawn + timeout/kill) with unix-only tests for success + command-not-found; gates green","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uhs","depends_on_id":"bd-37qz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:53:09.944944704Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uhs","depends_on_id":"bd-389q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:53:16.488550732Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3v08","title":"Message queue: core data model + delivery boundaries","description":"# Goal
Implement the internal **queue state machine** and define the delivery boundaries where queued messages become new agent inputs.

# Deliverables
- Data structures for queued messages:
  - kind: steering | follow_up
  - text
  - seq (monotonic) and/or timestamp
- Deterministic ordering (FIFO within each kind, global stable order).
- Identify delivery boundaries in the Rust agent/interactive integration:
  - after tool completion
  - after assistant completion
  - after abort

# Behavioral Spec (must match legacy)
- Steering: delivered after the current tool execution completes; cancels remaining tool iterations for that request.
- Follow-up: delivered only after the agent becomes fully idle.

# Implementation Notes
- Keep the queue logic isolated and testable (pure functions where possible).
- Define explicit “agent busy phases” so boundary detection is not heuristic.

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Queue semantics are implemented independent of UI.
- [ ] No deadlocks/races when messages are queued during streaming.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

 {"id":"bd-3v0j","title":"Epic: TUI Graduation — complete interactive mode features and remove WIP label","description":"# Goal
Complete the remaining interactive TUI features that are currently missing or incomplete, then remove the "WIP" designation from README.md and docs.

# Background
The interactive TUI (src/interactive.rs, 9,368 lines) is already substantially functional: full bubbletea-style architecture with viewport, text editor, autocomplete, spinner, model/thinking cycling, /slash commands, session management, keybinding customization, and theming. 90 tests pass for TUI state.

However, README.md still labels it "(WIP)" and several advanced features are missing:
1. Session branching has no visual UI — users cant see or navigate conversation branches
2. Edit tool results show raw text instead of colorized diffs
3. Images returned by read tool are not rendered in-terminal
4. Long-running tool executions have no progress indication beyond spinner
5. Tool output is not collapsible — long grep/find results push conversation off-screen

# Why This Matters
- "WIP" label on a core feature discourages adoption
- Session branching is a DOCUMENTED feature (README, Architecture) but invisible in the UI
- Inline diffs dramatically improve the "edit" tool UX — users can SEE what changed
- Image rendering in modern terminals (Kitty, iTerm2, WezTerm, Ghostty) is expected for 2026
- The comparison table in README shows "TUI rendering" as Pi advantage — it should be polished

# Architecture Notes
- Session branching UI: branch indicator in viewport header ("Branch 2/3"), Ctrl+B for branch picker (list model with tree visualization)
- Inline diff rendering: Use similar crate (already a dependency), render with lipgloss colors (green=add, red=remove, gray=context)
- Image rendering: Detect terminal capabilities via TERM/TERM_PROGRAM, Kitty graphics protocol (widest modern support), Sixel fallback, text placeholder for unsupported
- Progress indicators: For bash tool show elapsed time + output line count; for grep/find show file count scanned
- Tool output folding: collapse toggle per tool result block in viewport

# Children
1. Session branching UI (visual branch picker + navigator)
2. Inline diff rendering for edit tool results
3. Terminal image rendering (Kitty/Sixel protocol)
4. Tool execution progress indicators
5. Collapsible tool output blocks
6. Remove WIP labels from README/docs (after above items complete)

# Performance Constraints
- Image rendering must not block event loop — decode/encode in background task
- Diff rendering must handle files up to 10K lines without lag
- Branch picker must handle sessions with 50+ branches
- All new features must maintain 60fps rendering target","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"PurpleOwl","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:35:39.545998431Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:39:55.301720476Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["interactive","polish","tui"]}
 {"id":"bd-3v0j.1","title":"TUI: Colorized diff rendering for edit tool results","description":"Render edit tool diff from tool result details (details.diff) in interactive viewport with +/- coloring; include in loaded session history too.\n\nScope:\n- When tool output includes details.diff, append a Diff section and render lines with theme colors (add=success, remove=error, context=muted).\n- Preserve collapse behavior (global tool collapse toggle).\n- Add targeted unit/snapshot coverage for the formatter/rendering.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleOwl","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:45:47.455437244Z","created_by":"PurpleOwl","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:45:56.743654459Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["interactive","polish","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3v0j.1","depends_on_id":"bd-3v0j","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:45:47.455437244Z","created_by":"PurpleOwl"}]}
+{"id":"bd-3v0j.2","title":"Session branching UI — visual branch picker and navigator","description":"# Goal
Build the visual UI for session branching: a branch indicator in the conversation header, and a branch picker modal (Ctrl+B) that lets users see, navigate, and create conversation branches.

# Background
Sessions already support tree-structured branching (src/session.rs): each message has a parent field, enabling multiple children from any message (creating branches). The data model is complete. What is missing is any way for users to SEE branches exist or SWITCH between them in the interactive TUI.

Currently, the session just follows the "leaf" (most recent message on current path). If a user asks a question, gets an answer, then wants to try a different approach from the same question, they must start a new session entirely — even though the session format supports branching natively.

# Implementation Plan

1. Branch indicator (always visible):
   - In the viewport header bar, show: "Branch 1/3 at msg #15"
   - Calculate branch count by counting children of each fork point
   - Update indicator when user navigates branches

2. Branch picker modal (Ctrl+B):
   - List model (charmed_rust bubbletea) showing all branches
   - Each branch: preview of first message on branch, message count, timestamp
   - Tree visualization using box-drawing characters:
     msg #1: "Help me refactor..."
       ├── Branch 1 (current): "Sure, lets use..."  (12 msgs)
       ├── Branch 2: "An alternative approach..." (8 msgs)
       └── Branch 3: "What if we try..." (3 msgs)
   - Arrow keys to select, Enter to switch, Escape to cancel

3. Branch creation:
   - When user sends a message that creates a new branch (message from mid-conversation), show brief notification: "Branch 2 created"
   - Add /branch command to explicitly create a named branch

4. Navigation:
   - Ctrl+Left / Ctrl+Right: cycle between branches at current fork point
   - Conversation viewport reloads with selected branchs messages

# Files to Modify
- src/interactive.rs: Add BranchPickerModel, branch indicator, Ctrl+B handler
- src/session.rs: Add branch enumeration/navigation helpers (may already exist)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Branch indicator visible in header when session has branches
- [ ] Ctrl+B opens branch picker modal
- [ ] Branch picker shows tree structure with previews
- [ ] Enter switches to selected branch (conversation reloads)
- [ ] Ctrl+Left/Right cycles between branches
- [ ] Creating a branch shows notification
- [ ] Works correctly with sessions having 50+ branches","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:51.283941190Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:51.283941190Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["interactive","sessions","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3v0j.2","depends_on_id":"bd-3v0j","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:51.283941190Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-3v0j.3","title":"Inline diff rendering for edit tool results","description":"# Goal
When the edit tool returns results, render a colorized inline diff showing exactly what changed, instead of the current raw text "Successfully replaced X with Y".

# Background
The edit tool (src/tools.rs) returns a ToolOutput with a text description of the replacement. The similar crate (already a dependency) can compute line-level and word-level diffs. This task uses similar to generate diffs and lipgloss to render them with colors in the conversation viewport.

# Implementation Plan

1. Diff computation:
   - The edit tool already has the old text and new text
   - Use similar::TextDiff::from_lines() for line-level diff
   - For small changes (single line), use similar::TextDiff::from_words() for word-level highlighting

2. Diff rendering format:
   @@ src/main.rs (lines 42-45) @@
   -    let result = process(input);
   +    let result = process(input).await?;
        println!("{}", result);
   -    return result;
   +    result

3. Color scheme (lipgloss styles):
   - Red background: removed lines (-)
   - Green background: added lines (+)
   - Gray: context lines (unchanged)
   - Bold: changed words within a line (word-level highlighting)
   - File path + line range in header

4. Integration:
   - In the conversation viewport renderer, detect edit tool results
   - Replace raw text output with rendered diff
   - Keep raw text available via "show raw" toggle (for accessibility)

5. Truncation:
   - For large diffs (>50 lines changed), show first 20 + last 10 with "[...N lines...]" in between
   - Full diff available on expand

# Files to Modify
- src/tui.rs or src/interactive.rs: Diff rendering in conversation viewport
- src/tools.rs: Ensure edit tool output includes old_text and new_text for diff computation

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Edit tool results show colorized diff
- [ ] Added lines green, removed lines red, context lines gray
- [ ] Word-level highlighting for single-line changes
- [ ] File path and line range in diff header
- [ ] Large diffs truncated with expand option
- [ ] Raw text still accessible
- [ ] Diff rendering handles edge cases (empty old, empty new, whitespace-only changes)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:59:06.062780224Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:59:06.062780224Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["rendering","tools","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3v0j.3","depends_on_id":"bd-3v0j","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:59:06.062780224Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3w08","title":"PiWasm: Impl core polyfill (Module/Instance/Memory)","description":"# Goal
Implement the core WebAssembly polyfill surface in PiJS backed by wasmtime.

# Scope
- WebAssembly.instantiate(bytes, imports)
- WebAssembly.Memory (initial/maximum, grow, .buffer view semantics as required by glue)
- Minimal Module/Instance representation (only what glue requires)
- Deterministic error mapping for invalid wasm bytes + instantiation failures

# Constraints
- Generic only (no extension-id branching).
- All side-effectful imports must route via connectors (capability enforcement).

# Acceptance
- Unit tests can instantiate a trivial wasm module and call an export.
- Memory growth boundaries enforced (limits from policy/budgets).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:12:25.923613573Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T03:12:32.791524681Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3w08","depends_on_id":"bd-11cz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:12:32.791487011Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3w08","depends_on_id":"bd-1ry","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T03:12:25.930323990Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
